policy.batchDefaultMode: stateful fail-fast; use independent upstream_call calls for cross-server fan-out

checks.detail: Default adapter exposure is broker-first, not native projection-first.
Opt-in native projection defaults to safe projection only.
Brokered upstream calls validate the requested tool against current tools/list before forwarding.
Dynamic/hidden upstream tools require an explicit allowUnknownTool or operator-level escape hatch.
Tool title/description metadata injection patterns are policy-advisory risk signals.
Projected tool control arguments can pass explicit unknown-tool opt-in to brokered calls.
Tool exposure/call safety lifecycle is documented.
Adapter docs describe safe projection defaults and link to the tool safety contract.
A shared JSON-RPC envelope validator exists instead of ad-hoc per-route checks.
Requests must declare jsonrpc="2.0" before dispatch.
The method field is type-checked and cannot be empty.
Request IDs are restricted to JSON-RPC-compatible primitive forms.
Params are rejected when they are scalar values that cannot represent MCP request payloads.
stdio MCP server validates JSON-RPC envelope before matching methods.
Streamable HTTP route validates JSON-RPC envelope before dispatch/header contract handling.
stdio tools/call rejects non-object params.arguments.
HTTP tools/call rejects non-object params.arguments.
HTTP prompts/get also rejects scalar arguments instead of forwarding malformed payloads.
Brokered stdio upstream_call requires its nested arguments field to be an object.
Brokered HTTP upstream_call requires its nested arguments field to be an object.
Tuple batch calls reject scalar second elements.
Object-form batch calls reject scalar arguments.
HTTP tuple batch calls reject scalar second elements.
HTTP object-form batch calls reject scalar arguments.
The message-integrity audit is available as an npm verification command.

scenarioMatrix.expected: No file is written; output says dry-run-add.
scenarioMatrix.riskCovered: Prevents accidental config mutation while evaluating an MCP server.

scenarioMatrix.expected: First apply writes one fragment, second apply without --force fails, --force replaces.
scenarioMatrix.riskCovered: Prevents hidden reinstall/duplicate drift and makes replacement explicit.

scenarioMatrix.expected: Writes command/args only; does not execute the command during add.
Accepts http(s) URL and headers as config; rejects non-http(s).
scenarioMatrix.riskCovered: Separates remote domain ownership from local MCPace endpoint ownership.

scenarioMatrix.expected: Entry can be added with enabled=false; costs remain dependent on later runtime/tool calls.
scenarioMatrix.riskCovered: Avoids accidental activation while still allowing reviewable config.

scenarioMatrix.expected: 100 distinct fragments are written and visible in source inventory.
scenarioMatrix.riskCovered: Covers many-server config fanout without claiming runtime can safely run all concurrently.

Install output materialized command/args in JSON only; package execution is deferred until runtime/test/client launch.

exit=0 22ms

100 fragments written in 2128ms; inventory serverCount=104

observations: server install/add writes MCP settings fragments; it does not download packages or invoke upstream tools during registration.
npx-based presets defer package fetch/cache behavior until the command is later executed by server test, runtime, or a client.

Remote URL domains are upstream domains, not owned by MCPace unless the user controls that endpoint. MCPace serve.publicUrl is the advertised MCPace endpoint and must point to a user-controlled relay/domain when set.
100 configured servers is a config-scale scenario; it does not prove safe concurrent runtime launch of 100 expensive servers.
warnings: This smoke suite verifies registration semantics, not real package install latency, provider billing behavior, or live MCP tool calls.
Run live server tests only against reviewed servers with explicit credentials and cost limits.
